coredata.dc:title: Powder carbonization to synthesize novel carbon dots derived from uric acid for the detection of Ag(I) and glutathione
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Ju
Zhang, LiMei
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: This article first reported that a simple synthesis of carbon dots (UCDs) by pyrolysis of uric acid was proposed. The excitation wavelength was 350 nm and the emission wavelength was 402 nm for the synthesized UCDs. And the corresponding fluorescence quantum yield was 52.06%. The obtained UCDs could be served as a fluorescence probe to recognize Ag+ and glutathione (GSH), respectively. The fluorescence of UCDs was quenched after the addition of Ag+. The obtained UCDs had a linear relationship with Ag+ in the detection range of 0.1 μM to 2.0 μM, and the detection limit was 39 nM. The quenching fluorescence of this system could be restored after adding GSH. The fluorescence intensity increased linearly on increasing the concentration of glutathione in the range of 4 to 9 μM, and the detection limit was 66 nM. Based upon these phenomena, we proposed a novel fluorescence probe to detect Ag+ and detect GSH, respectively. In addition, the prepared UCDs were successfully applied to analyze Ag+ in mineral waters and GSH in blood serums.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uric acid
Carbon dots
Fluorescent switch
Ag+
Glutathione
The actual samples analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Quenching Effect of Ag+ Ions
Discuss the Reaction of Ag+ Ions and GSH to UCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of UCDs (Insert: diameter distribution histogram). (B) FT-IR spectrum of the gained UCDs. (C) XPS spectra of UCDs. (D) XPS spectra of C1s. (E) XPS spectra of O1s. (F) XPS spectra of N1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of UCDs. Insets showed the photographs of UCDs taken under visible light (a) and UV light (b). (B) The fluorescent spectra of UCDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) pH on the UCDs fluorescence intensity.

(A) Effect of UCDs with various metal ions. (B) Fluorescence spectra of UCDs upon addition of a series different concentration of Ag+ from 0 to 2 μM. (C) The relationship between △F and Ag+ concentration. (D) Effect of concentration of Ag+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of UCDs/Ag+ with different materials. Namely, Lys: L-Cysteine, GSH: glutathione, CH: cysteine hydrochloride, Glu: glucose, Lys: lysine, TsoH: p-CH3C6H4SO3H, Phe: phenylalanine, AA: ascorbic acid, His: histidine, EDTA-2Na: disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid, Lca: l-carnitine, AS: amikacin sulfate, NaH2PO4: sodium dihydrogen phosphate, KMnO4: mineral chameleon. (B) Fluorescence spectra of UCDs/Ag+ aqueous solution upon addition of a series of difference concentration of GSH from 0 to 10 μM. (C) The relationship between △F and Ag+ concentration.
Relative fluorescence intensity of the UCDs in the presence of different Ag+ and GSH.
Synthetic route for UCDs and the mechanism for Ag+ and GSH detection based on the fluorescent “on-off-on” system.
Comparison of different methods for Ag+ detection.
Comparison of different methods for GSH detection.
The analysis of the actual samples. (1–3: Ag+ in the mineral waters, 4–6: GSH in the human serums).

head.ce:title: Powder carbonization to synthesize novel carbon dots derived from uric acid for the detection of Ag(I) and glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory on Luminescence and Real-Time Analysis
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article first reported that a simple synthesis of carbon dots (UCDs) by pyrolysis of uric acid was proposed. The excitation wavelength was 350 nm and the emission wavelength was 402 nm for the synthesized UCDs. And the corresponding fluorescence quantum yield was 52.06%. The obtained UCDs could be served as a fluorescence probe to recognize Ag+ and glutathione (GSH), respectively. The fluorescence of UCDs was quenched after the addition of Ag+. The obtained UCDs had a linear relationship with Ag+ in the detection range of 0.1 μM to 2.0 μM, and the detection limit was 39 nM. The quenching fluorescence of this system could be restored after adding GSH. The fluorescence intensity increased linearly on increasing the concentration of glutathione in the range of 4 to 9 μM, and the detection limit was 66 nM. Based upon these phenomena, we proposed a novel fluorescence probe to detect Ag+ and detect GSH, respectively. In addition, the prepared UCDs were successfully applied to analyze Ag+ in mineral waters and GSH in blood serums.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver ions (Ag+ ions) have an oxidizing effect and are often used for sterilization in daily life [1]. Silver ions have attracted widespread attention due to their bioaccumulation characteristics [2,3]. Silver ions can bring about environmental pollution and pose the threat against biodiversity. Ag+ ions metabolites may cause a series of side effects such as suppress sulfhydryl enzymes, silver poisoning and silver accumulation [4,5]. For the sake of our environment and our health, silver ions are necessary to be detected. So far, many methods of detecting Ag+ ions have been developed, mainly including colorimetry, fluorescence, electrochemical methods and so on [6–11]. However, these methods are expensive, labor consumption and complex operation. Hence, the rapid and simple determination of silver ions has attracted much attention.

Glutathione (GSH) as a small molecule peptide consisting of three amino acids of glutamic acid (Glu), cysteine (Cys) and glycine (Gly), is intracellular thiol compounds [12]. Glutathione can help maintain normal immune system functions and has antioxidant effects [13,14]. Glutathione is distributed in animals and plants [15]. The normal concentration of glutathione in animal cells ranges from 0.5 to 10 mM. [16]. And the normal concentration of glutathione in physiological fluids ranges from 2 to 12 μM [17]. In addition, the abnormal indicators of glutathione can produce a series of adverse reactions, such as cancer, diabetes and other diseases [18–20]. For these reasons, glutathione is regarded as a reference index for detecting certain diseases. The main methods for determining glutathione are high performance liquid chromatography, fluorescence, electrochemical method and so forth [21–27]. The study of glutathione by carbon dots and silver ions is very rare.

In this paper, there is a quick and easy way to synthesize carbon dots through the pyrolysis channel using uric acid as the only raw material. The synthesized UCDs have been proved to be an effective fluorescent probe, which can detect Ag+ ions by fluorescence quenching and determine glutathione by fluorescence recovery. As shown in Scheme 1 , there is the synthetic route for UCDs and the mechanism for Ag+ and GSH detection. In addition, the prepared UCDs are used to determine Ag+ in mineral waters and GSH in blood serums.

Uric acid was come from China Pharmaceutical Shanghai Chemical Reagent Purchasing and Supply Station (Shanghai, China). AgNO3 was purchased from Guangzhou Jinhua Da Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Glutathione (GSH) was gained from Sangon Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai). All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical pure. And all the solutions were got by using distilled water.

The morphologies and microstructures of the synthesized UCDs were gained using the transmission electron microscopy (FEI Company, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) was used to determine the composition of the elements on the surface of UCDs. The excitation and emission spectra were recorded through the F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The absorption spectra were obtained from a Hitachi UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The surface structures of the UCDs were determined by the Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Kyoto, Japan).

The synthesis process of UCDs: uric acid (0.25 g) was placed in a crucible and heated at 225 °C for about 30 min under the air atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, dark brown solids were obtained. Then, 30.0 mL distilled water was added into the crucible. The acquired solution was ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Next, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min and further processed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove insoluble matters. The product was further purified in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1.0 kDa). Finally, the resulting solution was stored at 4 °C for the subsequent experiments.

Detection of silver ions: in a series of 10 mL colorimetric tubes, 200 μL of UCDs, 1 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer solution (pH = 7) and different concentrations of silver ions were added in sequence. Then, it was fixed to the scale line with distilled water. The fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

Detection of GSH: different concentrations of glutathione were added to the same concentration of UCDs containing 3 μM of silver ions (UCDs/Ag+). Then, the volume was made up to the mark line with distilled water. The corresponding fluorescence spectrum of mixed solution was recorded.
The mineral water samples were bought from the local store and used directly.
The human plasma samples were obtained from the local hospital. The human serum samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant of the actual samples were diluted 50 times [28].

The experimental conditions were the same as those of the above. Different concentrations of standard solutions were added to the actual samples and their corresponding fluorescence intensities were measured.

The UCDs were prepared by a pyrolysis powder strategy using uric acid as the reactant. The composition, morphology and surface functional groups of UCDs were characterized by means of X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and infrared spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 1 A, TEM image showed that the UCDs were scattered spherical, their size distribution ranged from 1.3 to 7.9 nm with an average particle size of 3.6 nm.

The chemical structure information of UCDs was known from the infrared spectrum. Fig. 1B expressed a NH stretching vibration absorption peak (3419 cm−1), a CC stretching vibration peak (1567 cm−1), a − C = 0 stretching vibration absorption peak (1663 cm−1), a CN stretching vibration absorption peak (1416 cm−1) and a CC stretching (1085 cm−1) [29–31].

XPS (Fig. 1C) displayed that UCDs were mainly composed of C, N and O elements, and their binding energies were located at 284.78, 339.88 and 532.5 eV, respectively. Analysis of C1s revealed three high-resolution peaks at 284.8, 286.0 and 288.7 eV, corresponding to CC, CN and CO bonds, separately (Fig. 1D) [32,33]. The O1S energy spectrum (Fig. 1E) showed one peak, which was attributed to C = 0 bonds (532.5 eV). The high resolution spectrum of N1S was shown in Fig. 1F, and the peak at 400.0 eV was NH groups [34].
The above series of experimental data showed that UCDs contained nitrogen elements.

The optical properties of UCDs were shown in Fig. 2A. The UCDs were colorless and transparent under sunlight, and emitted bright blue fluorescence under irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp. By analyzing the UV absorption spectrum, there were two absorption peaks. A strong peak at 306 nm was a π-π* transition of CO. The other relatively weak peak at 376 nm was the n-π* transition of the amine functional groups [35]. From the fluorescence spectra, we could see that the strongest emission peak of the UCDs was 402 nm (excited at 350 nm).

Next, the fluorescence emission spectra of UCDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–390 nm) were recorded. From the Fig. 2B, we could see that the emission spectra of UCDs were gradually red shifted. The reason for this phenomenon was related to both the different size of UCDs and the distribution of the surface state of UCDs [36,37]. And excited at 350 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of UCDs was located at 402 nm.

In order to study the relationship between pH and the fluorescence intensity of the UCDs, different pH B-R buffer solutions were added to the UCDs. As shown in Fig. 2C, it could be found that pH was 7, the fluorescence intensity of the UCDs was the strongest. Therefore, the pH was chosen to be 7 for the next experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Quenching Effect of Ag+ Ions
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the probe was investigated by adding a series of different metal ions to the UCDs (Fig. 3A). The metal ions were: 1 μM of Ag+, 200 μM of the different metal ions, including K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, and 1 μM of Hg2+ ions. The presence of Ag+ ions in UCDs led to a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity. While there were other metal ions in the UCDs, the fluorescence did not change significantly. Therefore, it was indicated that silver ions had a certain selective quenching effect on UCDs. This may be due to the formation of UCDs with amino functional groups under uric acid pyrolysis conditions, which could react with silver ions to form silver amine complexes [38,39].

The influence of silver ions with different concentrations on the fluorescence intensity of UCDs was investigated. With the increased of the concentration of silver ions in the solution, the fluorescence intensity of the UCDs gradually decreased (Fig. 3B). As shown in Fig. 3C, the concentration of silver ions in the range of 0.1 M to 0.45 M had a good linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching degree of UCDs (△F = 0.545C − 3.065, R2 = 0.990). The expression was: △F = F 0 − F 1, where F 0 and F 1 referred to the fluorescence intensity of the UCDs and UCDs/Ag+, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3D, we could conclude that the fluorescence quenching degree reached 98.5%, the Ag+ ions concentration was 3 μM. Therefore, the Ag+ ions concentration was set at 3 μM for further study. As shown in Table 1 , the method in this paper was compared with the methods reported in the literature to analyze performance from linear range and detection limit. We found that the detection limit of this method was not the lowest, but the synthesis of carbon dots in this paper was simple in process, cheap in raw materials and convenient in operation.

The selectivity of UCDs/Ag+ for a range of different substances had been studied. 11 μM of GSH, 200 μM of the other substances, including l-cysteine, cysteine hydrochloride, glucose, lysine, p-CH3C6H4SO3H, phenylalanine, ascorbic acid, histidine, disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid, l-carnitine, amikacin sulfate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, KMnO4 were added into the UCDs/Ag+ solution, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4A, we found that the quenched fluorescence intensity of UCDs/Ag+ was restored after adding glutathione, l-cysteine and cysteine hydrochloride, respectively. However, the addition of other substances did not significantly change the fluorescence intensity. It was found by analysis that glutathione, l-cysteine and cysteine hydrochloride all contained thiol functional groups. The recovery of fluorescence was due to the formation of AgS bonds by the thiol functional groups to the silver ions, thereby releasing the carbon dots. However, the addition of glutathione resulted in the most pronounced fluorescence recovery. Therefore, glutathione could be detected based on fluorescence recovery of UCDs/Ag+ system.

Next, the effects of different concentrations of glutathione on the fluorescence performance of the UCDs/Ag+ system were investigated. As the concentration of GSH increased, the fluorescence intensity of the UCDs/Ag+ system gradually recovered (Fig. 4B). When the concentration of glutathione reached a certain value, the fluorescence of quenching even returned to its original value. As shown in Fig. 4C, the concentration of glutathione in the range of 4.0 to 9.0 μM showed a good linear relationship with the degree of fluorescence recovery of the UCDs/Ag+. So, these results indicated that the UCDs/Ag+ detected glutathione through the fluorescence recovery channel. Table 2 compared the detection performance of the method provided in this paper with those reported in the literature. It was found that the detection limit of the method was comparable or lower than most of the literature methods previously reported.

The proposed fluorescent probe was used for detecting Ag+ ions in mineral drinks and GSH in human serums. Table 3 showed that the recovery of silver ions and glutathione in the samples were 98.7% to 105.5%, 97.4% to 102.5%, respectively. And the corresponding relative standard deviations were 1.4% to 2.2% and 2.5% to 5.4%, respectively. The above results revealed that the fluorescent method could be applied to the analysis of real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discuss the Reaction of Ag+ Ions and GSH to UCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of UCDs was analyzed in the presence of different concentration of Ag+ ions and GSH (Fig. 5 ). After the addition of silver ions, the fluorescence intensity of UCDs was obviously quenched. Comparing the effects of different concentrations of glutathione, the fluorescence intensity of the UCDs was not affected. However, the fluorescence intensity of the UCDs with Ag+ ions was restored after adding glutathione. In summary, the UCDs reacted with Ag+ ions to quench the fluorescence, and then the fluorescence intensity was restored after addition of glutathione.

In summary, we had succeeded in synthesizing UCDs with uric acid through a powder pyrolysis method. The synthetic UCDs emitted bright blue fluorescence. After the addition of silver ions, the fluorescence intensity of UCDs was obviously quenched. Then, the quenching fluorescence could be evidently recovered after adding GSH. The probe had been successfully used for the detection of actual samples. We think that this probe strategy is expected to be widely used in environmental analysis and food safety.
